```json
[
  "WID3002 - Natural Language Processing is a course taught by Dr. Mohamed Lubani.",
  "The content for the Natural Language Processing course was originally from Assoc. Prof. Dr. Norisma Idris.",
  "Language has been essential since early civilization.",
  "The 21st Century has experienced a \"Text Explosion\" due to the Digital Revolution and Internet Boom, leading to massive text generation, information overload, and unstructured, noisy text.",
  "Text preprocessing is an important, though often overlooked, step for NLP tasks that can increase accuracy if done correctly.",
  "Text preprocessing comprises steps to transform raw text into a predictable and analyzable format based on different tasks or applications.",
  "Text preprocessing takes raw text as input and returns cleansed tokens.",
  "All natural language is based on tokens, and complex natural language is built from more basic tokens.",
  "Tokenization is the process of breaking up a stream of text into words, phrases, symbols, or other meaningful elements called tokens.",
  "Paragraphs can be tokenized into sentences, and sentences can be tokenized into words.",
  "Word Tokenization breaks a sentence into words, while Sentence Tokenization splits text into sentences.",
  "A \"type\" is an element of the vocabulary representing unique words, a \"lemma\" refers to words with the same stem and part of speech, and a \"token\" is an instance of a type in running text.",
  "Issues in tokenization include handling punctuation in abbreviations (e.g., Mr., PhD.), compound words (e.g., Hewlett-Packard), and abbreviations/acronyms (e.g., AIDS).",
  "Naive splitting can fail with abbreviations, ellipses, and proper names, requiring sentence tokenization to learn patterns of sentence breaks beyond just punctuation.",
  "The `sent_tokenize` function uses a pre-trained `PunktSentenceTokenizer` and a statistical model to identify sentences accurately, even when abbreviations like \"U.N.\" are present.",
  "Lowercasing is one of the simplest and most effective forms of text preprocessing, reducing all letters to lower case.",
  "Lowercasing is useful for search engines and helps with consistency of expected output, especially with smaller datasets.",
  "Noise removal is an important step in Text Mining and NLP, especially when dealing with noisy texts such as social media comments.",
  "Noise removal includes methods like punctuation removal, special character removal, numbers removal, and HTML formatting removal.",
  "Removing HTML tags from web-scraped texts is beneficial as these tags are not useful for NLP tasks.",
  "Stopwords are common words that contribute little to overall meaning and are filtered out before further processing.",
  "Stopwords removal helps focus on important words and saves computing time and effort in processing large volumes of text.",
  "Stopwords are typically removed using pre-compiled lists.",
  "Stopwords removal is effective for applications like search engines or information extraction but may not be suitable for all applications, such as sentiment analysis where words like \"not\" are important.",
  "Stemming converts a word into its stem (root form) by eliminating its affixes (suffixes and prefixes), for example, reducing \"chocolates\" to \"chocolate.\"",
  "Lemmatization considers the context and converts a word to its meaningful base form, called a lemma, using a vocabulary and morphological analysis.",
  "Morphology is the study of words, how they are formed, and their relationship to other words in the same language.",
  "A morpheme is the small meaningful unit that makes up words, defined as the \"minimal unit of meaning.\"",
  "Morphemes are classified into stems (core meaning-bearing units) and affixes (prefixes, suffixes, infixes).",
  "A free morpheme can appear on its own as a word, while bound morphemes must be attached to a free morpheme.",
  "Morphological analysis helps reduce the number of unique words in a vocabulary by constructing words from more basic components.",
  "A stemming algorithm is a computational procedure that reduces all words with the same root to a common form by stripping derivational and inflectional suffixes.",
  "Stemming algorithms are categorized as Truncating (rule-based, e.g., Lovins, Porter), Statistical (e.g., n-Gram, Hidden Markov Model), or Mixed (corpus-based).",
  "The Porter Stemmer, developed by Martin Porter in 1980, is the most common truncating algorithm and is empirically effective for English.",
  "The S Stemmer is a simple truncating algorithm designed to handle pluralization by converting words to singular forms.",
  "Text normalization aims to replace non-standard tokens that carry significant meanings with context-appropriate standard words.",
  "Text normalization is important for noisy texts like social media comments, which often contain abbreviations, misspellings, and out-of-vocabulary words.",
  "Common approaches to text normalization include dictionary-based/lexicon-based methods and statistical machine translation (SMT).",
  "The level of text preprocessing needed varies depending on the domain, data volume, and data quality.",
  "Minimum preprocessing is sufficient for applications with abundant, well-written text in a general domain.",
  "More extensive preprocessing layers are helpful for narrow domains with sparse and noisy data (e.g., Tweets on restaurant reviews) to achieve better performance."
]
```
json_data: WID3002 - Natural Language Processing is a course taught by Dr. Mohamed Lubani.
The content for the Natural Language Processing course was originally from Assoc. Prof. Dr. Norisma Idris.
Language has been essential since early civilization.
The 21st Century has experienced a "Text Explosion" due to the Digital Revolution and Internet Boom, leading to massive text generation, information overload, and unstructured, noisy text.
Text preprocessing is an important, though often overlooked, step for NLP tasks that can increase accuracy if done correctly.
Text preprocessing comprises steps to transform raw text into a predictable and analyzable format based on different tasks or applications.
Text preprocessing takes raw text as input and returns cleansed tokens.
All natural language is based on tokens, and complex natural language is built from more basic tokens.
Tokenization is the process of breaking up a stream of text into words, phrases, symbols, or other meaningful elements called tokens.
Paragraphs can be tokenized into sentences, and sentences can be tokenized into words.
Word Tokenization breaks a sentence into words, while Sentence Tokenization splits text into sentences.
A "type" is an element of the vocabulary representing unique words, a "lemma" refers to words with the same stem and part of speech, and a "token" is an instance of a type in running text.
Issues in tokenization include handling punctuation in abbreviations (e.g., Mr., PhD.), compound words (e.g., Hewlett-Packard), and abbreviations/acronyms (e.g., AIDS).
Naive splitting can fail with abbreviations, ellipses, and proper names, requiring sentence tokenization to learn patterns of sentence breaks beyond just punctuation.
The `sent_tokenize` function uses a pre-trained `PunktSentenceTokenizer` and a statistical model to identify sentences accurately, even when abbreviations like "U.N." are present.
Lowercasing is one of the simplest and most effective forms of text preprocessing, reducing all letters to lower case.
Lowercasing is useful for search engines and helps with consistency of expected output, especially with smaller datasets.
Noise removal is an important step in Text Mining and NLP, especially when dealing with noisy texts such as social media comments.
Noise removal includes methods like punctuation removal, special character removal, numbers removal, and HTML formatting removal.
Removing HTML tags from web-scraped texts is beneficial as these tags are not useful for NLP tasks.
Stopwords are common words that contribute little to overall meaning and are filtered out before further processing.
Stopwords removal helps focus on important words and saves computing time and effort in processing large volumes of text.
Stopwords are typically removed using pre-compiled lists.

Stopwords removal is effective for applications like search engines or information extraction but may not be suitable for all applications, such as sentiment analysis where words like "not" are important.
Stemming converts a word into its stem (root form) by eliminating its affixes (suffixes and prefixes), for example, reducing "chocolates" to "chocolate."
Lemmatization considers the context and converts a word to its meaningful base form, called a lemma, using a vocabulary and morphological analysis.
Morphology is the study of words, how they are formed, and their relationship to other words in the same language.
A morpheme is the small meaningful unit that makes up words, defined as the "minimal unit of meaning."
Morphemes are classified into stems (core meaning-bearing units) and affixes (prefixes, suffixes, infixes).
A free morpheme can appear on its own as a word, while bound morphemes must be attached to a free morpheme.
Morphological analysis helps reduce the number of unique words in a vocabulary by constructing words from more basic components.
A stemming algorithm is a computational procedure that reduces all words with the same root to a common form by stripping derivational and inflectional suffixes.
Stemming algorithms are categorized as Truncating (rule-based, e.g., Lovins, Porter), Statistical (e.g., n-Gram, Hidden Markov Model), or Mixed (corpus-based).
The Porter Stemmer, developed by Martin Porter in 1980, is the most common truncating algorithm and is empirically effective for English.
The S Stemmer is a simple truncating algorithm designed to handle pluralization by converting words to singular forms.
Text normalization aims to replace non-standard tokens that carry significant meanings with context-appropriate standard words.
Text normalization is important for noisy texts like social media comments, which often contain abbreviations, misspellings, and out-of-vocabulary words.
Common approaches to text normalization include dictionary-based/lexicon-based methods and statistical machine translation (SMT).
The level of text preprocessing needed varies depending on the domain, data volume, and data quality.
Minimum preprocessing is sufficient for applications with abundant, well-written text in a general domain.
More extensive preprocessing layers are helpful for narrow domains with sparse and noisy data (e.g., Tweets on restaurant reviews) to achieve better performance.
